title: Tonys 2026: Salesman for Revival, Schmigadoon! Wins Musical

blocks.content: https://pyxis.nymag.com/v1/imgs/dd6/89e/d1d0992cb1c041a1b6b7204f2c96cc28a8-tonys-jellicle-ball.rhorizontal.w700.jpg

It’s the biggest night of the year for a small but extremely vocal percentage of the population, as New York’s theater community celebrates the dead salesmen, cake carriers, and Jellicle cats that defined this Broadway season at the 2026 Tony Awards. The non-equity Lost Boy known as P!nk is hosting the ceremony at Radio City Music Hall, kicking things off with an opening number penned by Evan Hansen’s two dads, Pasek and Paul, and featuring approximately 170 performers. From there on out, we’ll recap everything that happens during the ceremony while rooting for Vulture’s favorites to win. Becky Shaw in four! —Rebecca Alter
It’s amazing to end the Tonys by being reminded that Lorne Michaels really does sound like Dr. Evil.

Ragtime lyricist Lynn Ahrens got played offstage after the musical won Best Revival, then got interrupted while giving her speech backstage when the show’s Caissie Levy won for Best Actress in a Musical.
Schmigadoon! wins Best Musical.
Caissie Levy wins Best Performance by a Leading Actress in a Musical for Ragtime.
Joshua Henry wins Best Performance by a Leading Actor in a Musical for Ragtime.
Nathan Lane accepting in place of Scott Rudin.
No name-check for Rudin, either.
Death of a Salesman wins Best Revival of a Play.
Now maybe if Christopher Abbott had put Girls in his Salesman bio, things would have been different.
Alden Ehrenreich wins Best Performance by a Featured Actor in a Play for Becky Shaw.

“People can be skeptical when screen stars parachute into plays around Tony season to burnish their reputations,” Jackson McHenry wrote in an April Vulture profile of Ehrenreich. “A sentiment I’ve heard several times over in the last few weeks has run along the lines of, Alden Ehrenreich, who knew? And, How have they been hiding this guy from us in California?”
We were given a stern announcement not to clap during the ‘In Memoriam,’ which people of course rudely ignored
In the background of each photo in the ‘In Memoriam,’ there should be a Playbill or credit or some other hint at what these people are best known for.
Ragtime wins Best Revival of a Musical.
During Lesley’s whole speech, you could watch Rachel Dratch standing in darkness nearby onstage waiting to do her Rocky Horror narration.
They’re not playing people offstage, even when they get a li’l rambly.
I’m in favor of that. It’s a once-in-a-lifetime thing; let ’em gush a little.
Lesley Manville wins Best Performance by a Leading Actress in a Play for Oedipus.
The choreographer is back during the latest commercial break instructing everyone to the steps of the ‘Time Warp,’ which “you can do in your seat or standing up.”

It’s been a night of wonderful speeches, from Bess Wohl’s lovely tribute to the women in her life to Jellicle Ball co-directors Zhailon Levingston and Bill Rauch tag-teaming their thank-yous, but The Lost Boys’s Ali Louis Bourzgui elevated the form in a stirring diatribe where he spoke out against billionaires, fascism, and cruelty in the world.

He advocated for a free Palestine and acknowledged the legacy of Arab communities in theater. Like the vampires in his high-flying musical, he encouraged the audience to “find beauty in the ephemeral” in a speech that almost certainly won’t be his last on the Tonys stage.
Featured Actor in a Musical goes to Ali Louis Bourzgui for The Lost Boys.
I get that it’s hard to make a two-person musical live up to Cats, but that Two Strangers number felt so anticlimactic.
Someday, someone will figure out how to make prop cocktail glasses look realistically sloshy.
Today was not that day.
Is anyone clear on whether Two Strangers (Carry a Cake Across New York) is a romance or a friendship story?
I can’t figure out whether they fucked at the Plaza and whether I’m supposed to be sad that they don’t get together at the end.
John Leguizamo with the first ‘Go Knicks!’ of the night on the official broadcast.
THE CATS: JELLICLE BALL WEBSITE HAS CRASHED.
My predictions have been consistently wrong, but I am very happy so that has been worth it.
The advantage of making pessimistic predictions!
I just wanna say, ‘Why aren’t we all talking more about Bess Wohl?’
—S. Holdren, Vulture, February 20, 2025
Liberation wins Best Play.
It also won the 2026 Pulitzer for Drama.
If Liberation doesn’t take Best Play, I’m burning the place down.
The choreographer is back once again during a commercial break to tell people they’re not allowed to use the fans anymore. What a saga.
The Cats fans looked great on TV.
Shoshana Bean wins Best Performance by a Featured Actress in a Musical for The Lost Boys.
The choreographer is back and telling everyone to get out their Jellicle Ball fans and practice clacking with them during this commercial break.
A couple near me has a pair of fans but said they stole them from somewhere else in the orchestra. I am sadly in a fan-free section.
Joe Mantello wins Best Direction of a Play for Death of a Salesman.
Zhailon Levingston and Bill Rauch win Best Direction of a Musical for Cats: The Jellicle Ball.

The choreographer of the Tonys is onstage giving a speech during the commercial break, thanking Pasek and Paul and Mark Sonnenblick for writing the opening number and announcing that the audience is going to participate in a ‘My Heart Will Go On’ sing-along.

Watching from the audience at a distance, it was interesting how much more compelling the feed of the Ragtime number was on the screens that are on the side of the stage rather than the schematic movement on the stage itself.
Ragtime’s setlessness serves it in this format.
The camera can go anywhere.
If you’re wondering why Metcalf didn’t thank Salesman producer Scott Rudin: She probably knows she already went there and doesn’t exactly need to do it again on live TV.
Rudin relinquished his Broadway producing duties in 2021 following allegations of bullying but returned last season with a transfer of Little Bear Ridge Road, also starring Metcalf.
Laurie Metcalf wins Best Performance by a Featured Actress in a Play for Death of a Salesman.
This Schmigadoon! number is reminding me that when I saw the show, I spent a nonzero amount of time wishing I were just watching a really good production of The Music Man.
John Lithgow spoke to Vulture’s Rebecca Alter for a New York cover story about performing the most divisive role of the Broadway season.
My Roald Dahl is a broken clock who is correct twice a day. He says things that you reflexively agree with, and then he undermines it by betraying his own cruel nature.
John Lithgow wins Best Performance by a Leading Actor in a Play for Giant.

You’d think P!nk’s opening number would peak with her aerial stunts in full Peter Pan get-up, but the singer launched into a rendition of ‘Lady Marmalade’ that was basically like the Ariana DeBose BAFTA rap, but somehow … amazing?

Or maybe it’s that I’m going to be hearing “gitchie gitchie Lesley Manville” and “gitchie gitchie Laurie Metcalf” bouncing around my head for the rest of the night. If that weren’t enough, she got Megan Thee Stallion and Skimbleshanks out onstage with her for a rambunctious and joyful number that even had a Paddington cameo.
Well, of course the opening number killed in the room — especially loud cheering (from me) for Ann Harada.
Can she fly one more time, please?
Well, congratulations, P!nk, I will watch this thousands of times.
Lea Michele came for this???
This is just a successful version of what Ariana DeBose tried to do.
GITCHIE GITCHIE LESLEY MANVILLE GITCHIE GITCHIE CARRIE COON
“If you’ve been given a Cats fan, please put it away and take it out when our choreographer tells you how to use it.”
Seemingly a large portion of the orchestra received one, but not me!
P!nk is singing “Lady Marmalade” rewritten to name every woman in the room.
At the dress rehearsal today, P!nk and Neil had a really hard time getting those clips off.
Nailed it on the night!
An easy way to get people to root for P!nk is to put her up against Neil Patrick Harris.
I love that P!nk’s version of Peter Pan still has thigh-highs with crazy heels.
P!nk begins her hosting duties already suspended from the ceiling.
It’s pretty clear from what’s won so far that the night is going to go to what I’d call, with a little bit of a sigh, the safer bets.

Looks like Schmigadoon! and Death of a Salesman are going to walk away with lots of statues. And yes, I found Salesman extremely strong, and I also think it would be braver to give Becky Shaw that award. I can’t stop thinking about something my colleague Jason P. Frank said about Schmigadoon!, which is that it’s Extremely Competent.
Everyone is now in a giant clump trying to get to the bathrooms on Radio City’s basement level and back.

I nearly walked directly into Sam Hunter in the crowd, then realized there was no line for the elevators and beat the crowd by taking one. We are getting many stern announcements about getting back to our seats and clearing the aisles.
There is really so much Cats enthusiasm in the room, and they are all thrilled by its Choreography win.
Arturo Lyons and Omari Wiles win for The Jellicle Ball.
Best Scenic Design of a Musical goes to Dane Laffrey for The Lost Boys.
Chloe Lamford wins Best Scenic Design of a Play for Death of a Salesman.
Best Lighting Design of a Musical goes to Michael Arden and Jen Schriever for The Lost Boys.

When Jack Knowles won Best Lighting Design of a Play for Salesman, I heard cheering behind me and turned around to see Jack Falahee (of How to Get Away With Murder and who is also an understudy in Salesman) holding his phone out and taping the win like a proud mom, à la Amy Poehler in Mean Girls.
You could watch the mic slowly lowering as Laura Benanti walked offstage and Kristin Chenoweth walked on for her presentation.
Best Score goes to Cinco Paul for Schmigadoon!
Kai Harada wins Best Sound Design of a Musical for Ragtime.
Also nominated for Cats: The Jellicle Ball, Harada got to the stage and had no idea which show he won for.
Best Sound Design of a Play goes to Mikaal Sulaiman for Death of a Salesman.
Someone shouted “Laura, too soon!” when preshow host Laura Benanti made a joke about the length of her first two marriages.
We asked Clock Twink: Death of a Salesman Tony win or Knicks in 4?
Doug Besterman and Mike Morris win Best Orchestrations for Schmigadoon!
Schmig already off to an amazing start — obviously this was Cats’s award to win.
Cats fans clacking everywhere when Qween Jean won.
Qween Jean makes history as the first trans woman to win a Tony in any category.
Best Costume Design of a Play goes to Jeff Mahshie for Fallen Angels and Best Costume Design of a Musical goes to Qween Jean for Cats: The Jellicle Ball.
Jean was also nominated tonight for Best Costume Design of a Play for Liberation.
I can’t believe Best Book is in the preshow.
Cinco Paul for Schmigadoon! — it’s going to be Schmiga-night, I think.
Preshow announcement: “Please help us make this a safe and exciting opening number by being in your seats by 7:55. There’s going to be a lot of stuff in the aisles.”
Have made it through the insane security line and inside.
So far, I have seen Andrew Lloyd Webber taking a selfie with someone and Darren Criss bragging that he is out of Maybe Happy Ending so can now grow out his beard.
Three things I’m watching for at tonight’s Tonys

• How often will P!nk swing from the ceiling, and in which directions? We know she’s doing an elaborate opening number, but we don’t know just how much aerial gymnastics are involved.• Will there be any big upsets? Everyone I’ve talked to this weekend is biting their nails over close races, including the revival race between Cats and Ragtime, which seems to tilt toward the latter, though there are a lot of very enthusiastic fans of (and investors in) the former.• How celebratory will the mood be? Broadway’s facing some financial headwinds. The Tonys are a place to put on a brave face, but will the anxiety creep in?
